title: Rigid heddle honeycomb structure
subtitle: Using pick-up stick patterns to create a cellular textured surface
excerpt: Honeycomb weave on a rigid heddle loom uses a pick-up stick to create a pattern of raised and recessed cells across the cloth surface. The technique requires a pick-up stick and produces a textured, three-dimensional fabric suited to hand towels and scarves.
sourceNotes: Rigid-heddle honeycomb technique synthesised from Jane Patrick's The Weaver's Idea Book (2010, in copyright, substance only referenced) and general rigid-heddle pick-up pattern guides.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat stick threaded through the slot threads of a rigid-heddle warp to create a third shed. Turning the stick on its edge while the heddle is in neutral position opens a patterned shed that is otherwise inaccessible.
The combined reed and heddle of a rigid heddle loom. Hole threads are lifted or lowered with the heddle; slot threads hang free in neutral position.

A weave structure that creates a grid of raised and recessed rectangular cells on the cloth surface. The cells form because some threads float over several rows while adjacent threads weave normally, producing a quilted effect.
The threads stretched on the loom along the weaving direction. In rigid-heddle honeycomb, slot threads are picked up in a pattern to create the cells.

content.text: Honeycomb on the rigid heddle loom uses the
threaded through the slot threads of the
to open a pattern shed that the
block repeat requires four or six warp threads per cell and a sequence of five picks per cell row. Set up the pattern once and repeat it across the full warp width and the full length of the piece.

items.name: 8/2 cotton warp and weft, same or contrasting colour
Rigid-heddle loom, minimum 30 cm weaving width
Pick-up stick or flat shed stick, narrower than the loom

content.text: After warping at 7.5 dpi, place the heddle in neutral. Working along the slot threads from left to right, pick up every other group of 4 slot threads onto the pick-up stick: pick up 4, skip 4, pick up 4, skip 4, and so on across the full width. Slide the pick-up stick to the back of the loom where it sits out of the way during tabby rows and is pulled forward when the pattern shed is needed.

Each honeycomb cell block is woven in a repeating five-pick sequence. Pick 1: heddle up (hole threads raised). Pick 2: pick-up stick on edge in neutral heddle position (pattern slot shed). Pick 3: heddle up. Pick 4: pick-up stick on edge in neutral heddle position. Pick 5: heddle down (hole threads lowered). Beat all picks moderately. Repeat this sequence until the cells are square, typically 6 to 8 times per block, then shift the pick-up stick pattern by half a block to begin the offset cells.

items.problem: The cells do not appear in the finished cloth
items.cause: The pick-up stick shed was opened with the heddle up or down instead of in neutral position
items.fix: Check that the heddle is in the neutral (centre) position before turning the pick-up stick on edge. The pattern shed only works when the heddle is neutral.

items.problem: The cells are rectangular rather than square
items.cause: The five-pick sequence was repeated too few times before shifting
items.fix: Add more repeats of the five-pick sequence until the cells appear square when the cloth is measured off the loom. The exact number depends on the yarn weight and beat.
